turns.content: I'm trying to set up a workspace with multiple crates, but I thought the root Cargo.toml would automatically pull in every dependency from all sub-crates. It feels like it should just be one big list at the top.

Actually, that's not how workspace inheritance works. The dependencies defined in your root Cargo.toml are only inherited by crates that explicitly declare them via [patch] or [workspace.dependencies]. If a sub-crate doesn't have those declarations, it won't see the overrides unless you add them individually.
That makes sense. So if I want to update all versions across my entire workspace at once using cargo update, will that work even with inheritance?

Yes, cargo update respects the workspace configuration and can propagate changes through inherited dependencies. But you need to be careful because if a sub-crate relies on an older version of a dependency for compatibility reasons, blindly updating might break its build or tests.
What about security? Should I run cargo audit every time after an update?

Absolutely. Running cargo audit is a good habit, especially since vulnerabilities can creep in through transitive dependencies. You might also consider integrating cargo deny into your CI pipeline to enforce stricter policies on allowed crates and patch versions.
I'm worried about false positives though. Sometimes it flags something that's actually safe just because of a known issue in the registry. How do I handle those?

You can use cargo deny with custom policies to whitelist specific crates or versions, effectively ignoring certain false positives while still catching real risks. Think of it like a security guard who lets trusted friends pass but stops strangers at the door.

I'm coming from Java and I keep thinking that putting a workspace root in Cargo.toml is just like declaring a project, but what exactly is the difference between a regular crate configuration and using it as a workspace root? When I define [workspace] do I actually lose the ability to configure individual members?

The key distinction is that a workspace root acts as an aggregation layer for multiple crates rather than defining a single binary or library crate. When you use [workspace], you are telling Cargo that this directory contains a collection of packages, and the members inherit default settings like edition and resolver versions unless they override them in their own [package] tables. This means you don't lose configuration power; instead, you gain centralized management where you can still define specific overrides per crate if needed. Since you're exploring systems programming, have you considered using cargo deny or cargo audit to check your workspace for security vulnerabilities across all members at once?

I'm back at coding after five years, and I'm stuck on this workspace inheritance gotcha. My Cargo.toml defines a workspace with multiple members, but when I run cargo update, it seems to ignore some of the dependency versions defined in the individual crate configs. Here is my setup: [workspace] members = ["app", "lib"]
[package]
name = "app"
description = "My app"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"

[[dependencies]]
name = "serde_json"
version = "=1.0.95"

[workspace.dependencies]
serde_json = "1.0.95"

I thought the workspace-level override should apply to all members, but cargo update only updates the direct dependencies in the root crate.

That's a classic misunderstanding of how Cargo resolves transitive dependencies versus explicit overrides. The issue is that when you define [[dependencies]] inside an individual [package] section, Cargo treats it as a local constraint for that specific crate only. When you use [workspace.dependencies], those are metadata used by tools like cargo update or cargo audit to find versions across the tree, but they don't automatically override direct dependencies unless explicitly referenced. To fix this, you need to ensure your app's Cargo.toml includes serde_json from the workspace table directly in its own [[dependencies]] section using the workspace name, like: [workspace] members = ["app", "lib"]
[package]
name = "app"
description = "My app"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"

[dependencies]
serde_json.workspace = true

I'm building a tool to automate our build pipeline, and I thought that if we just add my crate to the workspace root Cargo.toml, it would automatically inherit all dependencies from every member crate. Can't I simply list them in the workspace config?

That's a common misunderstanding of how workspace inheritance works. Think of it like inheritance in object-oriented programming: adding a class to a parent doesn't magically copy all its properties unless you explicitly call `super` or use a specific inheritance pattern. In Rust, when you add a member crate to the workspace, it inherits the `[package]` metadata and default dependencies defined there, but not arbitrary dependency overrides from other members unless they are configured via `[patch]`. It's like saying "I inherit my father's house" but forgetting your father owns a separate garage that isn't automatically part of your address. You need to explicitly list dependencies in the root or use patch overrides if you want specific versions.
Ah, okay, so I can't just rely on automatic inheritance for version pinning? What if I run cargo update and it pulls in a breaking change from one of my member crates that I didn't intend to include?

Exactly. `cargo update` will only update dependencies defined in the specific crate or workspace root. If you have conflicting versions across members, running `cargo update` might pull in an unexpected version if not carefully managed. Imagine a family recipe book where each sibling writes their own version of a dish; just because your brother wrote a spicy version doesn't mean his version automatically becomes yours unless you explicitly choose to cook it that way. To prevent this, you should use `cargo deny` or `cargo audit` to scan for policy violations and ensure every dependency is audited before committing.

I see. So I need to run cargo audit first? But what if a member crate has an outdated dependency that the tool needs but isn't listed in my root config yet? How do I handle that without manually editing every file?

You can use `[patch]` sections in your workspace Cargo.toml to override specific dependencies for all members, acting like a global configuration switch. For example, you might write `[[patch."crate-name"]]` or simply add explicit dependency declarations in the root to force a version that works across the board. Think of it as setting a default password for every user account in an organization; even if individual users try to set their own, your policy enforces a consistent value. But before doing this, you should ask: are there any member crates with unique dependencies that shouldn't be overridden?

Good point. I have one legacy crate with an old dependency on serde_json::Value that we want to keep for compatibility, but the rest of the team wants to use newer serialization traits. How do I isolate that without breaking the build?

You can define a `[patch]` entry specifically for that legacy crate in your workspace root Cargo.toml to pin its dependencies while allowing others to evolve independently. This ensures `serde_json::Value` remains locked for that specific component without affecting the rest of the ecosystem. It's like having a special exception rule in an office building where only one floor is allowed to use old phones, while everyone else must switch to smartphones.
That makes sense. So my final workflow would be: audit with cargo deny, then apply selective patches via [patch] in the root?

Yes, that's a solid strategy. Start with `cargo deny` to identify policy violations, then use `[patch]` entries in your workspace root Cargo.toml to override specific problematic dependencies, ensuring your automated tool has consistent, audited versions across all member crates.
